summary: 39a recounts two parallel 40-year periods: Shimon HaTzaddik's miraculous service (lot always right hand, western lamp continuous, showbread fresh, incense smoke straight) and the 40 years before the Temple's destruction when all these signs reversed, culminating in Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai's prophetic rebuke of the self-opening Temple gates.

display.whats: Shimon HaTzaddik served 40 years; four miracles confirmed his righteousness.
display.hint: The lot, the western lamp, the showbread, and the incense smoke all showed miraculous signs during his tenure.
display.title: Shimon HaTzaddik's Forty Years of Miracles

learning.ahaMoment: Forty years of miracles - the lot, the lamp, the bread, and the smoke - each confirmed that Shimon HaTzaddik's service was accepted by God.
learning.memoryAnchor: Four miracles: right-hand lot, western lamp, fresh showbread, straight smoke - all during Shimon HaTzaddik's service.
learning.learnerQuestion: What four miraculous signs marked Shimon HaTzaddik's forty years of service, and why did they matter?
learning.coreTension: Ordinary priestly service could in principle produce the same outward results, lot, lamp, bread, smoke, by chance, yet the baraita treats their unbroken consistency across forty years as proof of something beyond chance.
learning.coreMove: The baraita lists all four signs together, then reports that all four ceased simultaneously upon Shimon HaTzaddik's death, using the abrupt and total reversal to argue the signs were miraculous, tied to him personally, not generic outcomes.

takeaway.text: During Shimon HaTzaddik's forty years, the lot always fell right, the western lamp stayed lit, the showbread stayed fresh, and the incense smoke rose straight, four signs the baraita reads as miraculous confirmation of his service, not coincidence.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may read each sign in isolation as a plausible natural outcome, missing that the baraita's argument rests on all four occurring together for forty straight years and stopping together at his death.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: Measuring naphtha: tell him to measure himself,
argumentFlow.text: Measuring naphtha: tell him to measure himself, not for you.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: Baraita: 40 years Shimon HaTzaddik served as
argumentFlow.text: Baraita: 40 years Shimon HaTzaddik served as KG.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: Sign 1: the Yom Kippur lot for
argumentFlow.text: Sign 1: the Yom Kippur lot for God came up in the right hand every year.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: Sign 2: the western lamp burned throughout
argumentFlow.text: Sign 2: the western lamp burned throughout the day.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: Sign 3: the showbread was warm when
argumentFlow.text: Sign 3: the showbread was warm when removed each Shabbat.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: Sign 4: the incense smoke rose straight
argumentFlow.text: Sign 4: the incense smoke rose straight up and never dispersed.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: After his death, these signs ceased: the
argumentFlow.text: After his death, these signs ceased: the lot came up in the left hand.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: The western lamp went out; the showbread
argumentFlow.text: The western lamp went out; the showbread was found crushed.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: The incense smoke began to disperse sideways.
argumentFlow.text: The incense smoke began to disperse sideways.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: The priests no longer blessed with the
argumentFlow.text: The priests no longer blessed with the full explicit Name.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: The crimson thread stopped turning white on
argumentFlow.text: The crimson thread stopped turning white on Yom Kippur.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: Gates of the Sanctuary opened by themselves
argumentFlow.text: Gates of the Sanctuary opened by themselves at night.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: R. Yochanan: the Temple's self-opening gates were
argumentFlow.text: R. Yochanan: the Temple's self-opening gates were a bad omen, fulfilled by Zechariah's prophecy.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: These signs signaled the coming destruction; Shimon
argumentFlow.text: These signs signaled the coming destruction; Shimon HaTzaddik's era was the last golden age.

misconceptions.misconception: These were standard, naturally expected results of the Temple service, not miraculous.
misconceptions.correction: The baraita's argument is built on the signs' total reversal immediately after Shimon HaTzaddik's death. If they were standard results of the service itself, they would have continued under his successors. Their simultaneous cessation is the Gemara's evidence that they were tied to him personally, not the routine.

visualizableElements.description: Four miracles: right-hand lot, continuous western lamp, fresh showbread, straight incense smoke

quizSeeds.question: What four signs occurred during Shimon HaTzaddik's tenure?
quizSeeds.answer: Right-hand lot every year, western lamp burning throughout, fresh showbread, straight incense smoke.

sugyot.finalRuling: During Shimon HaTzaddik's 40 years: lot always right hand, western lamp continuous, showbread fresh, incense smoke straight.

display.whats: Forty years before the Temple's destruction, further signs of decline appeared.
display.hint: 40 years before destruction: western lamp stopped, lot came left, gates opened themselves.
display.title: The Mirror Forty Years: Omens Before the Destruction

learning.ahaMoment: The 40-year countdown to destruction mirrors the 40-year era of Shimon HaTzaddik's miracles - same timeframe, opposite direction.
learning.memoryAnchor: 40 years miracles (Shimon) vs 40 years omens (pre-destruction) - same timeline, inverted meaning.
learning.learnerQuestion: What is the significance of a second forty-year period, this time of decline, appearing in the same baraita tradition?
learning.coreTension: Shimon HaTzaddik's forty years showed favor; the forty years before the destruction showed the same signs in reverse, raising the question of why the Gemara frames decline using the identical timeframe and the identical signs.
learning.coreMove: The baraita reports the lot coming up left, the lamp failing, and the gates opening by themselves, then has Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai rebuke the gates directly, reading their self-opening as the Temple's own admission of its coming fate, fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy.

takeaway.text: Forty years before the destruction, the lot came up left, the western lamp went out, and the Temple gates opened by themselves at night, the same sign-structure as Shimon HaTzaddik's era, inverted, which Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai read as a direct omen of destruction.

learning.learningBlocker: Learners may treat these omens as a list of disconnected ill signs rather than recognizing the deliberate parallel structure to the earlier forty years of miracles.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: Baraita: 40 years before destruction, the lot
argumentFlow.text: Baraita: 40 years before destruction, the lot for God came up in the left hand.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration: The western lamp went out and could
argumentFlow.text: The western lamp went out and could not be kept lit; the crimson thread stopped turning white.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai rebuked the Temple
argumentFlow.text: Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai rebuked the Temple gates: 'Heichal, Heichal, why do you alarm yourself?' - foretelling destruction.

misconceptions.misconception: The 40-year decline signs before the Temple's destruction were universally recognized and led to repentance.
misconceptions.correction: The signs were miraculous and unmistakable, yet they did not produce repentance. The Gemara records them as evidence of what was lost, not as a repentance narrative.

visualizableElements.description: 40 years of omens before destruction: lot left, lamp out, gates open

quizSeeds.question: What did Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai say about the Temple gates opening by themselves?
quizSeeds.answer: He rebuked them: 'Heichal, Heichal, why do you alarm yourself? I know your end will be destruction.'

sugyot.finalRuling: 40 years before destruction: lot came left, western lamp went out, gates opened - Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai foretold the end.

rashiTranslations.en: Measuring naphtha: if someone asks you to measure for him, tell him to measure himself - the context for the Shimon HaTzaddik discussion.
No person should touch what is designated for others.
Baraita: forty years Shimon HaTzaddik served as High Priest.
All forty years the Yom Kippur lot for God came up in the right hand.
This was a good omen: right hand = divine favor.
Sign 1: the lot for God always came up in the right hand.
Sign 2: the western lamp (ner ma'aravi) of the menorah burned all day without going out.
The western lamp was meant to burn continuously as proof of God's presence.
Sign 3: the showbread was warm and fresh when it was removed each Shabbat.
The showbread (lechem hapanim) stayed miraculously fresh all week.
Sign 4: the incense smoke rose straight up without dispersing.
Straight smoke = the incense was accepted above; it did not spread sideways.
After his death, these four signs ceased.
The lot began to come up in the left hand - an ill omen.
The western lamp went out and could not be maintained.
The showbread was found crushed or stale.
The incense smoke began to spread and disperse sideways.
The priests stopped pronouncing God's name explicitly in the blessing.
They began to use a substituted word (Adonai) instead of the Shem HaMeforash.
The crimson thread (lashon shel zehorit) stopped turning white on Yom Kippur.
In the time of Shimon HaTzaddik the crimson thread turned white reliably.
After his death it sometimes turned white, sometimes not, then stopped entirely.
The self-opening gates of the Sanctuary were a new and ominous sign.
Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai rebuked the gates: why do you open yourselves in alarm?
His rebuke: 'I know your end will be Zechariah's prophecy - destruction by fire.'
The gates' opening foreshadowed the Romans opening them by force.
Summary: 40-year golden era of Shimon HaTzaddik = 4 miracles; then reversal.
Baraita about 40 years before destruction: lot came left.
Western lamp went out consistently; crimson thread stopped turning white.
Temple gates opened by themselves each night.
Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai interpreted this as prophetic fulfillment.
Zechariah: 'Open your doors, O Lebanon, that fire may consume your cedars.'
Lebanon = the Temple (white as snow); fire = Roman destruction.
The signs of decline mirrored the earlier signs of divine favor.
The 40-year period of omens is the mirror of Shimon HaTzaddik's 40 years.
Both periods teach that Temple service directly reflects the nation's spiritual state.
When the nation is worthy, miracles abound; when not, signs of withdrawal appear.
The Gemara preserves these traditions to teach about the nature of divine relationship.
The Temple's fate was written in these signs before the destruction came.
Rabban Yochanan ben Zakkai's rebuke shows prophetic foreknowledge.
He understood that the self-opening gates were the Temple's own alarm.
The Heichal knew its fate before the people did.
Summary: 40 years of Shimon HaTzaddik's miracles vs 40 years of pre-destruction omens.
These traditions frame the entire Yom Kippur service within cosmic history.
The lot, the lamp, the bread, the smoke - each speaks to divine presence.
Their cessation speaks to divine withdrawal.
The Gemara's historical narrative enriches the halachic Yom Kippur service.
39a concludes with the historical backdrop for the Yom Kippur avodah.
The service is performed in hope of restoration of these miracles.
Each year's Yom Kippur is a prayer for the return of Shimon HaTzaddik's era.
The theological stakes of Yom Kippur are embedded in this historical narrative.
Summary of 39a: measuring naphtha, 40-year Shimon HaTzaddik miracles, 40-year omens.
Two time periods: one of miraculous favor, one of ominous withdrawal.
Both measured in 40 years; both teach about the Temple's relationship with heaven.
39b continues with more details about the signs and the lot ceremony.
The lot discussion resumes with the Yom Kippur service narrative.
The transition from historical digression back to halacha occurs in 39b.
The Gemara returns to the lot ceremony after the Shimon HaTzaddik narrative.
Both historical and halachic elements serve the same goal: understanding Yom Kippur.
